summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone SE as a device that combines the beloved 4-inch form factor of the iPhone 5/5S with iPhone 6S-class performance and camera quality. He argues that the smaller form factor genuinely feels better in hand — flat sides, no camera bump, true one-handed usability — and that its industrial design from 2012 remains utterly modern in 2016. While acknowledging trade-offs like a smaller display, slower Touch ID, no 3D Touch, and a 64 GB storage cap, he finds the SE compelling, especially at its groundbreaking $400/$500 price points. Ultimately, he admits he's torn but leans toward the SE, even though he expects the 4-inch size will soon be relegated to second-tier status when the iPhone 7 arrives.
key_points: The iPhone 5-style industrial design is the first to span three product generations, and its 2012 form factor remains modern and relevant in 2016
The SE's biggest practical advantages are one-handed usability, better grip from flat sides, no camera bump, and a significantly lower price — saving a full third over a comparable 6S
The most surprising downside is increased typing errors on the smaller keyboard, which could be a deciding factor for heavy typists
The SE is essentially a 6S in performance and camera quality, with only minor spec differences (slower Touch ID, lower contrast ratio, no 3D Touch, no barometer)
Gruber expects Apple will not keep the 4-inch form factor at top-tier specs, making the SE a temporary pinnacle for small-phone lovers
money_quotes: Feel matters. And to me, the classic 4-inch display form factor shared by the iPhones 5, 5S, and now SE feels the best in hand.
I absolutely loathe the camera bump on the 6/6S; the lack of said bump on the SE feels downright luxurious in contrast.
It truly says something that an industrial design first introduced in 2012 remains utterly modern and relevant in 2016.
The iPhone SE is a credible one-handed device. The iPhone 6/6S, not so much. The iPhone 6/6S Plus, not at all.
You save an entire third of the price by choosing a 64 GB iPhone SE over a 64 GB 6S.
No 3D Touch. After almost two weeks of using the SE, I almost forgot to mention this.
Me, I talk the talk about preferring the smaller form factor, but ultimately I'm a sucker for top-of-the-line CPU/GPU performance and camera quality.
And then I pick up the iPhone SE, and hold it in my hand.
key_insight: The iPhone SE proves that superb industrial design and one-handed usability have lasting value, but Apple's relentless upgrade cycle means small-phone lovers face an expiration date on top-tier specs in their preferred form factor.
